advantages: RMSE and MAE are in the same units as y — directly interpretable (dollars off, degrees off) without extra scaling.
RMSE is differentiable in ŷ when used as a loss surrogate and aligns with MSE-trained models; as a metric it highlights large errors stakeholders care about.
MAE is robust to outliers and heavy-tailed residuals — a stable readout of typical error when a few labels are wild.
Both metrics decompose over examples, so you can audit per-region or per-segment RMSE/MAE for debugging.
Universally supported in sklearn, PyTorch, R, and Kaggle — easy to compare against published benchmarks.
disadvantages: RMSE and MAE ignore error direction — over- and under-predictions with the same magnitude score identically.
Neither metric captures business-asymmetric costs (e.g., under-forecasting inventory is worse than over-forecasting).
RMSE is sensitive to outliers — a few bad labels can dominate the reported number and mislead model comparison.
MAE under-penalizes rare catastrophic misses — two models with equal MAE can differ sharply in tail risk (visible in RMSE).
Scale-dependent: RMSE/MAE on raw dollars vs log-dollars are not comparable without consistent target transforms.
